coredata.dc:title: Sesame-derived ions co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for bio-imaging, sensing and patterning applications
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Caiyan
Xuan, Tongtong
Yan, Dong
Lou, Sunqi
Hou, Xian
Chen, Yiwei
Wang, Jing
Li, Huili
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) are promising fluorescence probes with potential applications in sensors, bio-imaging, photo-catalysis, ion detection, fluorescent inks and other areas, mainly due to their high stability, low toxicity and cost-effectiveness. However, the relatively low quantum yield (QY) and complex synthesis routes usually hinder their applications. Here, highly efficient FCNPs doped with N, P and Ca ions have been prepared via a simple hydrothermal process using green natural raw materials of sesame as precursors. The products emit strong blue fluorescence with QY as high as 48.5% under 360nm excitation due to the improved electronic characteristics and abundant defect sites induced by natural N, P and Ca ions co-doping. The bright fluorescence, high photostability, ultralow toxicity and good biocompatibility endow as-prepared FCNPs with great potential applicatios in bio-imaging and patterning. Most importantly, as-prepared FCNPs exhibit an extraordinarily high sensitivity and selectivity to Fe3+ ions compared to other metal ions in aqueous solution. The fluorescence intensity is inversely proportional to the concentration of Fe3+ ions, and the calibration curve displays linear regions over the range of 0–90nM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.5nM. Meanwhile, it shows a “turn-on” fluorescence response to F− with good linearity at 10–60nM and a LOD of 2.4nM. These results indicate that FCNPs, as a fluorescent sensing platform, could meet the selective requirements for biomedical analysis and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles
High quantum yield
Bio-imaging probes
Fe3+ and F− ions detection
Fluorescent inks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell toxicity test and cell bio-imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of formation and applications of FCNPs derived from sesame.
UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (λex =360nm) of the FCNPs synthesized at (a) different reaction temperature for (b) 1–5h with (c) various precursor concentration.

UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of FCNPs under varying excitation wavelengths. The top inset exhibits the corresponding normalized PL curves; the lower inset displays photos taken under daylight and 365nm UV light, respectively.
(a) TEM image, (b) histogram and Gauss fitting of size distribution, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of FCNPs.
XPS survey spectrum of the FCNPs (a), magnification and fitting curves of C1s (b), O1s (c), N1s (d), P2p (e), and Ca2p (f) peaks.

(a) Effects of the FCNPs on HeLa cell viability. Cell bio-imaging images of HeLa cells incubated with FCNPs (0.5mg/mL) for 2h: (b) bright-field image, (c) confocal fluorescence microphotograph, and (d) merged bright-field and fluorescence image.
Optical and fluorescent images of mung bean sprouts: grown in water under daylight (a), grown in an aqueous suspension of FCNPs (2mg/mL) under daylight (b) and 365nm excitation (c).

(a) PL quenching curves of FCNPs upon exposure to various metal ions. (b) PL quenching response of FCNPs to various concentrations of Fe3+. (c) PL recovery response of FCNPs+Fe3+ mixture to different anions. (d) PL recovery response of the FCNPs+Fe3+ mixture to F− with various concentrations. The insets of (b) and (d) are the linear region of the sensing response.
Calligraphy of FCNPs fluorescent inks with a writing brush on filter paper. Photos were captured under (a) daylight and (b) 365nm UV light excitation, respectively.
Stern-Volmer constant (K sv) measured under different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Sesame-derived ions co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for bio-imaging, sensing and patterning applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics & Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Materials Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics & Advanced Instrument
School of Physics and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic and Synthetic Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies, KLGHEI of Environment and Energy Chemistry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic and Synthetic Chemistry
State Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies
KLGHEI of Environment and Energy Chemistry
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) are promising fluorescence probes with potential applications in sensors, bio-imaging, photo-catalysis, ion detection, fluorescent inks and other areas, mainly due to their high stability, low toxicity and cost-effectiveness. However, the relatively low quantum yield (QY) and complex synthesis routes usually hinder their applications. Here, highly efficient FCNPs doped with N, P and Ca ions have been prepared via a simple hydrothermal process using green natural raw materials of sesame as precursors. The products emit strong blue fluorescence with QY as high as 48.5% under 360nm excitation due to the improved electronic characteristics and abundant defect sites induced by natural N, P and Ca ions co-doping. The bright fluorescence, high photostability, ultralow toxicity and good biocompatibility endow as-prepared FCNPs with great potential applicatios in bio-imaging and patterning. Most importantly, as-prepared FCNPs exhibit an extraordinarily high sensitivity and selectivity to Fe3+ ions compared to other metal ions in aqueous solution. The fluorescence intensity is inversely proportional to the concentration of Fe3+ ions, and the calibration curve displays linear regions over the range of 0–90nM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.5nM. Meanwhile, it shows a “turn-on” fluorescence response to F− with good linearity at 10–60nM and a LOD of 2.4nM. These results indicate that FCNPs, as a fluorescent sensing platform, could meet the selective requirements for biomedical analysis and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, due to their low toxicity, excellent bio-compatibility, low cost and favorable water solubility, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) have garnered tremendous research interest in a wide range of applications, such as ion detection [1,2], bio-imaging [2], fluorescent inks [3], medical diagnosis [4] and photo-catalysis [5].

Since FCNPs were first discovered by Scrivens’s group in 2004, various methods, which can be classified as top-down or bottom-up approaches [6–9], have been attempted to prepare FCNPs. In top-down methods, FCNPs can be prepared by peeling carbon nanoparticles from large carbon structures (e.g., diamond [8], graphite [9], activated carbon [10], and carbon nanotubes [11]) via arc discharge [8], electrochemical processes [11], or laser erosion [12]. These techniques are usually based on special sophisticated equipment, rigorous conditions, or toxic reagents, which limit the mass production of FCNPs and their further practical applications. Bottom-up approaches rely on the carbonization of carbohydrates [13], amino acids [14], and some small molecules [15,16]. These methods provide more convenient pathways than do top-down methods and have achieved remarkable progress for the synthesis of FCNPs. However, the quantum yield (QY) of the obtained FCNPs is generally low, which cannot satisfy their further applications. Therefore, it is still urgent and necessary to synthesize FCNPs with high fluorescence.

Heteroatoms doping is one effective approach to enhance the surface activity and electrical properties of FCNPs and thereby improve their QY and fluorescent performances [17–19]. B-doped FCNPs with an improved QY of 15% were fabricated via microwave heating of citric acid, boric acid and urea by Bourlinos’s group [17]. Xu’s group reported that N, S co-doped FCNPs prepared from sulfamide and sodium citrate achieved a QY of 55% [18]. Shen et al. obtained B, N co-doped FCNPs with a high QY of 50% by a hydrothermal approach using boric acid and ethylenediamine as starting materials [19]. Although the above-described significant progress has been made in the preparation of heteroatoms doped FCNPs with a high QY, these approaches may lead to environmental issues, high costs and the generation of toxic products. Therefore, the fabrication of ion doped FCNPs via a facile environmental approach with nontoxicity and low-cost raw materials still remains a challenge.

Recently, some studies have reported that green natural materials containing heteroatoms, such as rice [20], wool [21], pigskin [22], orange juice [23], leeks [24], hair fiber [25] and bombyx mori silk [26], were adopted as carbon sources to replace chemical reagents to obtain low toxicity ion-doped FCNPs via bottom-up methods. For example, Shi et al. synthesized N-doped FCNPs with a QY of 22.5% via the pyrolysis and subsequent microwave treatment of wool [21]. Their research group also reported a rapid hydrothermal method that uses pigskin to produce N-doped FCNPs with a QY of 24.1% [22]. N, S co-doped FCNPs with a QY of 11.1% derived from hair fibers were also fabricated via a surface oxidation and carbonization method with sulfuric acid by Zhang’s group [25]. These encouraging studies proved that bottom-up approaches with green natural materials containing abundant heteroatoms as carbon sources are successful strategies to enhance the QY and bio-compatible performance of FCNPs [21,22]. However, the obtained fluorescence still cannot satisfy the requirements of their further application, and some of these methods require a surface oxidation process with chemical reagents to improve their water solubility and fluorescence [22,26], which may introduce toxic reactants into FCNPs and thus limit their applications in biological and environmental fields. Therefore, the preparation of high-quality non-toxic FCNPs with applicable performances and strong fluorescence from green natural precursors by simple bottom-up methods is still a significant challenge but is well worth further efforts.

Sesame is a green, cheap and nourishing food that consists of abundant C, N, O, P and Ca element owing to its inherent carbohydrate, protein, lecithin, vitamin E, fat and Ca components. This material is expected to be an effective precursor for preparing ion co-doped FCNPs without any extra chemical additives. However, such a strategy has not been reported to date. In this work, FCNPs co-doped with N, P and Ca ions were obtained via a simple hydrothermal treatment of sesame in pure water, and they show a high QY of 48.5% under an excitation wavelength of 360nm, using quinine sulfate as a reference. Combining the facile green approach, excellent fluorescence, high water solubility, good bio-compatibility and ultralow toxicity, the as-prepared FCNPs show satisfactory applicable performances in bio-imaging, ion detection and fluorescent inks.

Sesame was collected from a farm and triturated by ball milling for further use. The mung bean sprouts were purchased from a vegetable market for further labeling. Quinine sulfate, K2Cr2O7, FeCl2, FeCl3, CoCl2, NiCl2, AgNO3, MnCl2, KCl, SnCl4·5H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, AlCl3, ZnCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, HgCl2, MgCl2, H2SO4, NH4Cl, (NH4)2SO4, NH4NO3 and NH4F were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8), and HeLa cells were purchased from Qiaodu Bio-technology Co., Ltd. Dialysis membrane was purchased from To-science Co., Ltd. All chemicals were of AR grade and used as received without other treatments.

First, triturated sesame powder (0.05–0.5g) was mixed with 6mL of distilled water under vigorous magnetic stirring. Next, the mixture solution was transferred into a 10mL autoclave and heated, varying from 150 to 200°C for 1–5h. Finally, FCNPs in a clear brown aqueous suspension were obtained after the filtering process using micro-porous filter paper (0.22μm) and the subsequent purified pathway with dialysis membrane (1000Da) for 48h for further characterization.

UV–vis spectrophotometry (Hitachi U-3900) and fluorescence spectrophotometry (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-4) were employed to capture the absorption and photoluminescent (PL) properties of the FCNPs. The relative viability of cells was assessed by analyzing the absorbance of solution at 450nm using a micro-plate reader (Multiskan MK3, Thermo). Confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss LSM 700) was applied to obtain fluorescence photographs. The QY of the FCNPs was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Q
Y x
= Q
Y R
I x
I R
A R
A x
n x 2
n R 2
The subscripts “x” and “R” refer to FCNPs and quinine sulfate, respectively. We use quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as a reference (QYR
=54% under λex =360nm, and nR
=1.33) [22,27]. I is the PL intensity, A is absorbance, and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The FCNPs were dissolved in pure water with nx
=1.33. The absorbance values of all samples were kept below 0.10 to avoid reabsorption. X-ray diffractometry (XRD, M21XVHF2Z, Mac Science Co., Ltd.) was carried out to explore the phase structure of the FCNPs. A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL) was employed to observe the morphology and size distribution of the products. A Raman spectrometer with a 632.8nm He-Ne laser as the irradiation source (RM-1000, Renishaw) was used to analyze their composition and structures. A BI-200SM multi-angle dynamic/static laser scattering instrument (Brookhaven, USA) was adopted to record the hydrodynamic size of the resultant FCNPs in distilled water. The chemical environment, content, oxidation state, and functional groups of the FCNPs were characterized by multifunction imaging photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) and a Nicolet Nexus 600 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet Instrument Co., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell toxicity test and cell bio-imaging

ce:section.ce:para: The CCK-8 assay on HeLa cells was adopted to investigate the toxicity of the as-prepared FCNPs. First, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 1 × 104 cells per well in DMEM with 10% FBS, and then the cells were incubated at 37°C under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 for 24h. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with 100μL of fresh medium, which contained different concentrations of FCNPs (0∼2mg/mL). After the medium was removed for a certain time (24 or 48h), fresh medium containing CCK-8 was added to each well, and the cells were incubated for 4h. Finally, the relative viability of cells was assessed via a micro-plate reader.

First, HeLa cells were plated in DMEM with 10% FBS. Then, after culturing in humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37°C for 12h, the medium was replaced with fresh DMEM, which contained FCNPs at a concentration of 0.5mg/mL. Next, the cells were incubated for 2h, and they were then rinsed with phosphate buffered saline to remove excess FCNPs. Finally, fluorescence images of the cells were obtained via a confocal laser scanning microscope under excitation of 405nm.

The detection of ions was performed according to the following procedure. First, 0.5mL of each aqueous solution of the relevant ions (Cr6+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Mn2+, K+, Sn4+, Cu2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Hg2+ and Mg2+) with the same concentration (200nM) was added into 3.5mL of an aqueous suspension of FCNPs (0.5mg/mL) with 5min of mixing at room temperature. In the subsequent PL recovery study, 0.5mL of each aqueous solution of relevant anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and F−) with the same concentration (300nM) were added into the resultant mixed solution of FCNPs+Fe3+. The PL measurements were recorded under excitation at 360nm.

Filter paper was chosen as writing paper, which has been proven to be a suitable substrate with no background fluorescence under a UV lamp for FCNPs to adhere to. The FCNPs aqueous suspension (0.5mg/mL) was chosen as a fluorescent ink. The desired words were written onto a piece of the filter paper by hand with a writing brush. The fluorescent images were obtained under excitation (λex =365nm) with a hand-held UV lamp.

Scheme 1 outlines the procedure employed to synthesize FCNPs and their further applications. It is well known that sesame contains protein, lecithin, fat, vitamin E, carbohydrate and Ca, which consists of abundant C, N, P, O and Ca elements. After a simple hydrothermal process in pure water, uniformly dispersed FCNPs can be clearly observed, and the N, P and Ca from protein, lecithin and Ca are expected to replace C atoms in the C skeleton, forming the N, P and Ca ion co-doped FCNPs. Moreover, the obtained ions co-doped FCNPs show strong blue luminescence, and we thus hope the as-prepared FCNPs derived from this food grade material show the possibility to be used as probes for bio-imaging, ion detection and fluorescent inks through this simple and green treatment.

To obtain FCNPs with optimal fluorescence, the temperature and reaction time of the hydrothermal process and the concentration of sesame in the aqueous solution were optimized, and the results are depicted in Fig. 1 . The absorption intensity is enhanced with the increase of hydrothermal temperature, reaction time and sesame concentration. However, the maximum value of the corresponding emission intensity is under the experimental conditions of 180°C for 3h with a sesame concentration of 50mg/mL, which means that FCNPs prepared under this condition own the best optical properties.

The optical properties of FCNPs prepared at the optimized experimental conditions are shown in Fig. 2 . The as-prepared FCNPs exhibit one absorption band at 280nm, which should be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the CO band [27–29]. For the emission spectra at varying excitation wavelengths, FCNPs present the strongest excitation and emission peaks at 360nm and 438nm (Fig. 2), respectively, and show a bright blue light under a UV lamp (bottom inset of Fig. 2). Moreover, upon changing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 420nm, the maximum PL emission peak of shifts from 416 to 486nm (top inset of Fig. 2). Such an excitation-dependent PL property of the FCNPs is in good accordance with previous reports [3,13,19,20]. Encouragingly, the as-prepared FCNPs show a high QY of 48.5% when using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as a reference (Fig. S1), which reaches an excellent standard compared with that of the reported FCNPs [20–22]. The high QY may be ascribed to the abundant defect emissive sites, an improvement of the electronic characteristic and surface chemical activity induced by synergetic doping with multiple ions [17–19,27,28]. The corresponding explorations and discussions will be conducted in the following sections.

The morphology of FCNPs was measured by TEM, as shown in Fig. 3 a. FCNPs present a spherical shape with uniform dispersion and have a mean particle diameter of 25nm (Fig. 3b), which is smaller than 40nm observed hydrodynamic diameters in distilled water (Fig. S2). The hydrodynamic diameter is the diameter of the particle plus ligands, ions or molecules that is associated with the surface and travels with the particle in solution. These ligands, ions or other associated molecules make the particle appear larger than TEM result. This phenomenon was also observed in our previous work [41]. Fig. 3c shows the XRD profile of the FCNPs, in which one weak broad peek appears at approximately 24.2°, corresponding to the (002) diffraction mode of the carbon structure with an interlayer spacing (d) of 0.41nm, which is larger than that of graphite (0.34nm) [27,28].The enhancement of the d value may be attributed to the introduction of O-based groups into carbon, and thus lead to poor crystallinity of the FCNPs [27,28]. To further identify the structure of the FCNPs, the Raman spectrum was studied, as shown in Fig. 3d. The Raman spectrum shows two peaks at 1365cm−1 and 1580cm−1, corresponding to the defect-induced band (D) and the crystalline graphite band (G), which are attributed to sp3 and sp2 carbons, respectively [27,29]. It is well known that the coexistence of D and G bands indicates an amorphous carbon structure of FCNPs [27,28], which is consistent with the above XRD results.

The chemical environment and composition of FCNPs were explored by XPS, as depicted in Fig. 4 . Binding energies were calibrated with that of C1s at 284.8eV as a reference. In Fig. 4a, five dominant peaks at 133.4, 284.8, 346.7, 399.8 and 532.0eV are ascribed to P2p, C1s, Ca2p, N1s and O1s, respectively [28–31]. In addition, the contents of C, O, N, P and Ca in the FCNPs are 66.39, 26.08, 1.88, 1.4 and 4.25 at.%, respectively. To further analyze the elements in the FCNPs, high-resolution XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 4b–f. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b), four peaks at 284.6, 285.1, 286.1 and 288.7eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CP, CO and CO species, respectively, are observed [27,32,33]. The O1s spectrum presents two types of O bonds: CO at 532.4eV and CO at 533.2eV (Fig. 4c) [21,27]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) shows two peaks at 399.4 and 400.3eV, which correspond to pyridinic N (NH) and pyrrolic N (NH), respectively [27]. Previous studies have indicated that pyrrolic N exposes planar edges or defect sites where they reside, and pyridinic N is formed by replacing C atoms with N on defect sites or edges of the plane [34]. These defect sites and edge planes induced by N doping can dramatically enhance the surface chemical activity and electronic characteristic of the FCNPs. The P2p spectrum (Fig. 4e) can be divided into two peaks approximately at 133.1 and 133.9eV, corresponding to PC/PN and PO species, respectively [30,32,33]. Moreover, it has been proven that P doping can replace corner in the carbon skeleton to form PC and PN bonds, which favors the n-π* transition and thus further improves their electronic performance and surface activity [30,32,33]. As shown in Fig. 4f, two peaks at 347.9eV (Ca2p3/2) and 351.5eV (Ca2p3/1) are observed in the Ca2p spectrum, which verifies the existence of Ca2+ in the FCNPs [35,36]. It is noted that the position of the above two peaks is slightly higher compared with that of the corresponding Ca2p signals (347.0eV) in CaO, which is mainly due to their different coordination environments. This strongly proves the successful chelation of Ca2+ on the surface of the FCNPs [35]. Kim and Hussain’s groups proved that Ca2+ can bind strongly on the defect sites in the carbon skeleton with good stability, and the introduction of Ca2+ in carbons can modify their electron density and supply some active sites [36]. Qu et al. suggested that the “metal-cation-functionalization effect” in FCNPs can decrease the overlap between absorption and emission, leading to a small self-absorption, which can improve their fluorescence emission [37]. These results all indicate that the as-prepared FCNPs are composed of C, O, N, P and Ca. The multiple ions co-doping can supply abundant defect sites and functional groups, improve the electronic characteristic of the FCNPs, and as a result, imparts excellent water solubility and outstanding fluorescent properties to the FCNPs.

To further confirm the types of functional groups that are present on the FCNPs, the FTIR spectrum was collected. As shown in Fig. S3, the characteristic absorption bands at 3250–3490cm−1 is assigned to NH and OH stretching vibrations [27]. The bands at 2830–2950cm−1 correspond to the vibration of CH. The peaks at 1430–1618cm−1 indicate the presence of symmetric/asymmetric stretching vibrations of CO [27,28,38]. The bands at 1208–1370cm−1 correspond to the CN group [27,39]. The stretching vibration bands of CO and PO at approximately 1038–1170cm−1 are due to the presence of carboxyl and other oxygen-containing functional groups [32,39]. Several weak peaks (640–810cm−1) can be assigned to P-aromatic stretching of PC bonds [32]. These results further prove that the obtained ion co-doped FCNPs consist of functional groups on their surface, which is well consistent with the XPS results. The presence of these functional groups impacts the remarkable hydrophilicity and stability of the FCNPs in an aqueous system.

Beyond the above mentioned properties of the FCNPs, their fluorescence stabilities under different conditions were further investigated in detail, as shown in Fig. S4. When FCNPs are freely and easily dispersible in distilled water, good photo-stability is exhibited. PL intensity and appearance remain unchanged after storage for 100days in air at room temperature (Fig. S4a). Fig. S4b manifests the independence of PL intensity on the exposure times under UV irradiation (365nm). That is to say, the FCNPs are highly resistant to photo-bleaching. Additionally, their emission behavior is independent of the ionic strength of the medium and the fluorescence intensity remains constant with varying NaCl concentrations in the aqueous solution (Fig. S4c). All results demonstrate the excellent fluorescence stability of the as-prepared FCNPs.

The application of FCNPs as probes for bio-imaging was studied, as shown in Figs. 5 and 6
. Fig. 5a shows the experimental cell toxicity results of FCNPs using HeLa cells via CCK-8 assay for 24 or 48h with different FCNPs concentrations. Obviously, the FCNPs scarcely impose toxicity to the HeLa cells, and the corresponding cell viability is above 90%, even at a high FCNPs concentration (2mg/mL) with a long incubation time (48h), which indicates good bio-compatibility between the FCNPs and HeLa cells. The fluorescence images of living HeLa cells tagged with 0.5mg/mL FCNPs for 2h are shown in Fig. 5b–d. Fig. 5b shows the control bright field image of HeLa cells without fluorescence. After a single 405nm laser excitation, strong blue fluorescence induced by the FCNPs appears in the phase contrast image (Fig. 5c). To make sure that the above observed blue fluorescence is indeed from FCNPs, we further conducted one control experiment under the same conditions without the incubation of FCNPs in the HeLa cells, as shown in Fig. S5. No fluorescence was observed in this control, which confirms that the obtained blue fluorescence is attributed to the FCNPs attached to HeLa cells rather than the auto-fluorescence of these cells. Furthermore, Fig. 5d presents the merged image with bright-field and fluorescence images, in which PL signals are only observed in the cytoplasmic area of HeLa cells instead of in the nucleus. This indicates that FCNPs can successfully enter the HeLa cells and rarely penetrate into the cell nucleus, which can effectively avoid the genetic disruption of HeLa cells. Moreover, no structural damage appears in the HeLa cells after exposure to FCNPs, which indicates the ultralow toxicity and good bio-compatibility of the FCNPs, consistent with the results shown in Fig. 5a.

Given their ultralow toxicity and feasibility in cell imaging, we further studied the toxicity and application of FCNPs as bio-imaging probes in plants, as shown in Fig. 6. First, some mung bean sprouts were soaked in FCNPs aqueous suspension (2mg/mL) for 48h. Similar to the phenomenon of mung bean sprouts grown in water (Fig. 6a), no anomalies are found in the mung bean sprouts grown in the FCNPs aqueous suspension (Fig. 6b). Under UV lamp irradiation (λex =365nm), mung bean sprouts exhibit strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 6c), which demonstrates that the FCNPs can successfully penetrate into the plant cells with a negligible effect on the plant growth. These results all indicate that the FCNPs exhibit good bio-compatibility and strong blue fluorescence as bio-imaging probes. The excellent applicable performance of FCNPs should be ascribed to the nontoxic raw material, the green synthetic process and the incorporation of natural ions co-doping, which can balance the good bio-compatibility and strong fluorescence of FCNPs.

To extend the application of the FCNPs, PL intensity quenching experiments were first performed by using the FCNPs as probes for the detection of different metal cations (Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr6+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Mn2+, K+, Sn4+, Cu2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, and Mg2+) at the same concentration (200nM). These ions mainly related to environment. As shown in Fig. 7 a, Fe3+ exhibits the highest relative PL intensity quenching ability among various metal ions, which indicates that FCNPs own a higher resolution for Fe3+ detection. Previous reports have proven that the PL intensity quenching ability of FCNPs is mainly attributed to the formation of a charge transfer complex with the combination of metal cations and COOH functional groups on the surface of FCNPs [33,39–44]. Therefore, in our work, the excellent selectivity of the prepared FCNPs for Fe3+ should be ascribed to the faster chelating process between FCNPs and Fe3+ through COOH group to most effectively form charge transfer complex [33,40,41,45]. In addition to selectivity, sensitivity is another indispensable parameter to evaluate the performance of probes in the sensing field. Hence, different concentrations of Fe3+ were added into the aqueous solution of FCNPs and the change in fluorescence intensities at 438nm was measured and presented in Fig. 7b. Obviously, the PL intensity of the FCNPs decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, which indicates the sensitivity of the FCNPs to the Fe3+ concentration. In further, the inset in Fig. 7b calculates the dependence of I/I0 on the concentration of Fe3+ (I0 and I are the PL intensities of the FCNPs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively). A good linear correlation (r2 =0.991) can be observed over the concentration range of 0–90nM, indicating the high efficiency of the FCNPs as probe to detect Fe3+ in water.

Dynamic quenching and static quenching are two basic forms of fluorescence quenching. Quenching originating from collisional interaction between the fluorophore and quencher is called collisional or dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation [45–49]:
(2.1)
I0/I = τ0 /τ =1+ K qτ0[Q]
where I0 and I represent the steady-state luminescence intensities in the absence and presence of quenchers, respectively, K q is the bimolecular rate constant describing the efficiency of the collisional encounters between the luminophore and the quencher, reflecting the rate constant of dynamic quenching; τ0 and τ are the excited state luminescence lifetime in the absence and presence of the quenchers, and [Q] is the quencher concentration in solution; Static quenching occurs as a result of the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between the ground state of fluorophore and quencher. For this type of quenching, the decrease of fluorescence intensity has the following form of the Stern-Volmer equation [49–54]:
(2.2)
I0/I =1+ K sv[Q]
in Eq. (2.2), Ksv is now the association constant KS.

As reported [47–50], the lifetime of the fluorophore is not affected by the static quenching, while absorption curve and K sv at different temperatures are usually perturbed by the static quenching. Hence, lifetime, absorption curves, and K sv measurements are definitive methods to distinguish between static and dynamic quenching. The corresponding results are exhibited in Figs. S6 and S7, and Table 1 . Lifetime study shows that in the absence and presence of Fe3+, the lifetime of FCNPs remains unchanged at 3.89ns, suggesting that in this case, the mechanism of the quenching process involves complexation (static quenching) rather than collisional deactivation (dynamic quenching). Absorbance curves (Fig. S7) varying with the Fe3+ concentration also indicates that the quenching in this work is static. In order to verify the above mechanism of the quenching process in further, the quenching constants (K sv) for FCNPs with Fe3+ at different temperatures were calculated by the Stern-Volmer method and are listed in Table 1. The results show that the Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching constant K sv is inversely correlated with temperatures, which once more indicate that the probable quenching mechanism of fluorescence of FCNPs by Fe3+ is not initiated by dynamic collision but compound formation. In other words, the fluorescence quenching of FCNPs by Fe3+ results from complex formation is predominant, while from dynamic collision could be negligible. Based on the results, the lowest limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was estimated to be 1.5nM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which is three orders of magnitude lower than the maximum tolerance (5.4mM) in drinking water permitted by the US Environmental Protection Agency and precious reports [47,55]. Fe3+ is important for many pathological and physiological processes, and abnormal content of Fe3+ in body may cause severe diseases, such as hepatitis, diabetes, Parkinson's disease and cancers. Therefore, the development of FCNPs with low toxicity and high efficiency for Fe3+ ion detection in this study exhibits great application potential in biological analysis [40,56].

To further explore the selectivity and sensitivity of the FCNPs as probes for anion detection, we performed a PL intensity recovery study using different anion aqueous solution (F−, NO3 −, SO4 2− and Cl−) with the above obtained FCNPs+Fe3+ mixing solution, as shown in Fig. 7c and d. The mixing solution of FCNPs and Fe3+ is responsive towards F− with high sensitivity and precision (Fig. 7c and d and inset of Fig. 7d). PL intensity of the mixing solution of FCNPs and Fe3+ increases with the concentration of F−. A good linear correlation (r2 =0.9857) between I0/I and F− concentration can be observed in the range of 10–60nM, indicating the high efficiency of the mixing solution of FCNPs+Fe3+ to F−. As a result, the LOD (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) for F− is estimated to be 2.4nM, which is at least one order of magnitude lower than that of previous reports [57,58]. The reason should be ascribed to the good stability and insolubilization of the formed FeF3 compound in water [40,42,43]. Previous researches have proven that FeF3 is quite stable but FeCl3 is soluble and both FeNO3 and FeSO4 are difficult to form in water [40,42,43,59,60]. F− is not only considered as water pollutants, but also plays a vital role in biological system. The excess intake of F− in body may irreversibly damage the teeth, bone and kidney [40,44,57–60]. The maximum permissible limit of F in drinking water is set at 1.5mgl−1 by the World Health Organization, while this value is 1.0mgl−1 in China [57,58]. In view of these facts, the continuous monitoring of the presence and movement of F− is of great importance and has vast research value to treat clinical diseases and protect the environment. Hence, it is very significant and important to successfully prepare FCNPs as probes in this work with highly selective and sensitive detection for F− ion in water.

The advantages of high PLQY, easily reproducible preparation and excellent dispersibility in water endow the FCNPs with broader application prospects, for example, in the form of UV-actived fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting, which is undetectable under visible light. For this purpose, we used as-prepared FCNPs aqueous suspension (0.5mg/mL) as a fluorescent ink for writing phrase “China Dream” with the corresponding Chinese characters and the acronym “ECNU” of “East China Normal University”. As shown in Fig. 8 , the calligraphy was achieved on commercially available filter paper using a Chinese writing brush by hand (inset of Fig. 8a). All characters are invisible in daylight and no auto-fluorescence appears from the filter paper (Fig. 8a), but clear words with strong blue fluorescence are observed (Fig. 8b) under the UV lamp (λex =365nm). Obviously, the strong PL signals come from the FCNPs ink with high photoluminescence. It is suggested that the water-soluble FCNPs can be used fluorescent ink to replace traditional inks safely forming clear, permanent, adelomorphic fluorescent graphic and text for application in the field of anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and information storage.

In summary, this work describes a green, cheap and facile method to prepare high-quality FCNPs via a one-step hydrothermal process using the green natural material sesame without any chemical reagents. The resultant FCNPs show strong blue fluorescence with QY as high as 48.5% under 360nm excitation, mainly due to their improved electronic characteristics and abundant defect sites induced by natural N, P and Ca ion co-doping. Meanwhile, the FCNPs present ultralow toxicity, high aqueous stability, excellent bio-compatibility and especially high selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ and F− ion detection. The fluorescence intensity versus Fe3+ and F− ions concentration is a good linearity in a large range. These results suggest that the FCNPs are attractive candidates as probes for bio-imaging, ion detection, anti-counterfeit, biomedical analysis, and environmental monitoring.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51472087), the ECNU Reward for Out-standing Doctoral Dissertation Cultivation Plan of Action (No. PY2015041), and Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Transparent Opto-functional Inorganic Materials, Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KLTOIM201606).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Differentiation and determination of metal ions using fluorescent sensor array based on carbon nanodots

A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Dual-emission carbon nanodots as a ratiometric nanosensor for the detection of glucose and glucose oxidase

Triple-mode emission of carbon dots: applications for advanced anti-counterfeiting
Preparation of carbon quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence by rapid laser passivation in ordinary organic solvents
Carbon nanodots made on diamond surface by focused ion beam
Microwave-hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from graphite oxide
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light

Controllable synthesis of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from bombyx mori silk-natural proteins
One-step self-assembled epoxide-containing nanodots as an enzyme-immobilized platform for biosensing
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
Photophysical properties of doped carbon dots (N P, and B) and their influence on electron/hole transfer in carbon dots-nickel (II) phthalocyanine conjugates

P-doped porous carbon as metal free catalysts for selective aerobic oxidation with an unexpected mechanism

Nitrogen-doped carbon microspheres derived from oatmeal as high capacity and superior long life anode material for sodium ion battery
Calcium doped graphane as a hydrogen storage material
Effect of vacancy defects in graphene on metal anchoring and hydrogen adsorption
Toward efficient orange emissive carbon nanodots through conjugated sp2-domain controlling and surface charges engineering
Gadolinium-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield as an effective fluorescence and magnetic resonance bimodal imaging probe
Facile synthesis of highly luminescent co-doped carbon nanodots for rapid sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+
Starch (Tapioca) to carbon dots: an efficient green approach to an on-fff-on photoluminescence probe for fluoride ion sensing

Antimicrobial activity of metals: mechanisms: molecular targets and applications
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
Zr(H2O)2EDTA modulated luminescent carbon dots as fluorescent probes for fluoride detection
Combined static and dynamic quenching in micellar systems-closed-form integrated rate laws verified using a versatile probe
A fluorescence turn-off chemosensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
Determination of the stern-volmer constant in quantum systems with dual fluorescence

Fluorescent conjugated polymer molecular wire chemosensors for transition metal ion recognition and signaling
Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper (II) ions
A plastic optical fiber sensor for the dual sensing of temperature and oxygen
Lanthanide-doped ceria nanoparticles as fluorescence-quenching probes for dissolved oxygen
A fluorescence turn-off chemosensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution

Signaling recognition events with fluorescent sensors and switches
The absorption spectra of ferric compounds. I. The ferric chloride-phenol reaction

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Caiyan Yu received her MS degree in 2014 at Henan University. Currently she is pursuing her PhD degree under the supervision of Prof. Huili Li at East China Normal University, China. Her research interests mainly focus on the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for applications in bio-imaging, ion detection and fluorescent inks.

Tongtong Xuan received his PhD degree from East China Normal University in China in 2016. Currently, he is an associate professor in School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University. His research interests mainly focus on the production of semiconductor quantum dots and perovskites nanocrystals, and their applications for white LED, solar cells and bio-imaging.
Dong Yan received his MS degree in 2014 at Henan University. Now he is pursuing his PhD in East China Normal University. His research interest is the preparation and application of the nanomaterials.

Sunqi Lou received his Bachelor's degrees in 2014 at Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology. Now he is pursuing his MS degree under the supervision of Prof. Huili Li at East China Normal University, China. His research interest is the synthesis and application of semiconductor quantum dots.

Xian Hou received his MS degree from Northwest Normal University in China in 2014. Currently he is pursuing his PhD degree at East China Normal University, China. His research interests mainly focus on semiconductor quantum dots.

Yiwei Chen, senior engineer, Bachelor of engineering, at the East China normal university department of physics, engaged in the preparation and characterization of nanomaterials. He is proficient in the technology of electronic microscope analysis.

Jing Wang is a professor in School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University. He received his PhD in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy Of Sciences in 2004. His research focuses on the synthesis and applications of phosphors and semiconductor nanocrystals, and their applications for white LED, bio-imaging, etc. He has published over 50 articles in renowned international journals, and filed over 10 Chinese patents.

Huili Li is a professor of Department of Physics at East China Normal University. She received her PhD in Materials Science and Engineering from Shanghai Institute of Ceramics (SICCAS), Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2006, then worked as a Research Fellow in National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS) (Japan) (2006–2008). In 2009, she joined in the Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics & Advaced Instruments, Department of Physics, East China Normal University, by Talent Introduction. Her research focuses on the synthesis, properties, and applications of high efficient inorganic phosphors, colloidal semiconductor and metal nanocrystals, and their applications for ion detection, white LED, FED, solar cells, bioimaging, etc. She has published over 50 articles in renowned international journals, and filed over 9 Chinese patents.
